title: Back-of-House Order: Storage and Organisation That Scales
subtitle: A tidy back of house is not about aesthetics. It is about speed, safety, and the ability to find what you need when it matters.

metrics.label: maximum time to find any item in storage

sections.heading: Why Back-of-House Matters
sections.body:
Customers never see your back of house. That is exactly why it deteriorates. Without the pressure of customer eyes, storage areas, prep zones, and staff spaces gradually descend into chaos. Boxes pile up. Shelves overflow. The system — if there ever was one — collapses under the weight of daily urgency.

This matters for three reasons.

**Speed.** During a rush, every second counts. If a barista needs to restock oat milk and it takes ninety seconds to find it in a cluttered storeroom, that is ninety seconds of lost service time. Multiply that by every restock, every shift, every day, and disorganisation is costing you hours per week.

**Safety.** A cluttered back of house is a hazard. Boxes blocking fire exits. Heavy items stored above head height. Cleaning chemicals stored next to food. Wet floors with no signage. Food safety inspectors check your back of house, and a messy one signals a business that is not in control.

**Morale.** Staff spend significant time in back-of-house areas — prep, storage, breaks, cleaning. A chaotic, dirty, or uncomfortable back of house tells them that their working environment does not matter. A well-organised, clean space tells them you care about the whole operation, not just the customer-facing parts.

The standard for back of house is simple: everything has a place, everything is in its place, and everyone knows where everything goes. It sounds basic because it is basic. But basic does not mean easy — it requires initial setup, ongoing maintenance, and a refusal to let standards slip when things get busy.

The best test is this: could a new team member, on their first shift, find everything they need without asking? If not, your back of house needs work.

sections.heading: Storage Area Organisation
sections.body:
Effective storage follows a few simple principles. Apply them consistently and your storeroom becomes a tool instead of an obstacle.

**Zone your storage areas:**

Divide your storage into clear zones. Use labels, shelf markers, or colour-coded areas.

- **Dry goods zone:** Coffee beans, sugar, syrups, flour, dry ingredients. Keep off the floor (minimum 15cm clearance for pest prevention and cleaning). FIFO rotation — new stock at the back, old stock at the front.
- **Chilled zone:** Walk-in fridge or under-counter fridges. Organise by category: dairy together, meats together, prepared items together. Raw items always below ready-to-eat items to prevent drip contamination.
- **Frozen zone:** Clearly labelled with contents and date frozen. Nothing should be in the freezer for more than three months without a specific reason.
- **Packaging and disposables:** Cups, lids, bags, napkins, takeaway containers. Store near the service area to minimise distance during restocking.
- **Cleaning supplies:** Separate from all food storage. Locked or access-controlled if required by your risk assessment. COSHH data sheets accessible.

**The labelling system:**

Every shelf should be labelled with what goes there. Not what is currently on it — what should be on it. This distinction matters because it creates accountability. An empty shelf labelled "Oat Milk (par: 12 cartons)" tells the closer that stock needs ordering. An unlabelled shelf tells them nothing.

**Par levels:**

Set par levels for every item in storage. The par level is the minimum quantity you should have on hand before reordering. Write it on the shelf label. When stock drops below par, it goes on the order list. This prevents both stockouts and overstocking.

Use T148-01 to map your storage layout and set par levels. Review quarterly — seasonal changes affect demand, and par levels should shift accordingly.

**The monthly audit:**

Once a month, walk your entire storage area with a critical eye. Remove expired items. Consolidate partial containers. Check that everything is in its designated zone. Wipe down shelves. This takes thirty minutes and prevents the slow creep of disorder that turns a tidy storeroom into a mess over six months.

sections.heading: Sustainability Features
sections.body:
Sustainability in your physical environment is not about virtue signalling. It is about reducing operating costs and meeting the expectations of an increasingly environmentally aware customer base.

**Energy efficiency:**

Lighting, heating, and equipment are your biggest energy costs. Start with the easiest wins:

- Replace all lighting with LED. The payback period is typically under twelve months, and LEDs last five to ten times longer than traditional bulbs.
- Install a programmable thermostat. Heating an empty cafe overnight or over-cooling in summer wastes money. Set schedules that match your trading hours with a thirty-minute pre-heat buffer.
- Turn off equipment when not in use. An espresso machine left on overnight uses significant energy. If your machine takes fifteen minutes to heat up, build that into the opening routine rather than leaving it on twenty-four hours.
- Check door seals on fridges and freezers annually. A worn seal lets cold air escape and forces the compressor to work harder.

**Water conservation:**

- Fix dripping taps immediately. A dripping tap wastes up to fifteen litres per day.
- Use a commercial dishwasher rather than hand-washing where possible — modern dishwashers use less water per item.
- Review your steam wand purging habits. Excessive purging wastes water and energy.

**Waste infrastructure:**

Make recycling easy for your team by providing clearly labelled bins in the back of house: general waste, mixed recycling, food waste, cardboard, and coffee grounds. If recycling is inconvenient, it will not happen.

Coffee grounds can be collected by local composting schemes, offered to customers for garden use, or picked up by companies that repurpose them (such as local composting or repurposing services).

Use T153-01 to audit your current sustainability features and prioritise improvements by cost-to-benefit ratio. Quick wins first, capital investments later. Track your utility bills monthly to see the impact of changes — visible savings motivate continued effort.

sections.heading: Interior Design Refreshes
sections.body:
Your cafe's interior ages invisibly. You see it every day, so you stop noticing the scuffed walls, the faded artwork, the wobbly table that everyone avoids. But customers notice. New customers especially notice — they are assessing your cafe on first impression, and a tired interior says tired business.

A full refit is expensive and disruptive. But a refresh — targeted, affordable, regular — keeps your space feeling current without closing for weeks.

**The annual refresh cycle:**

Use T154-01 to plan a refresh each year. Budget one to three percent of annual revenue for interior maintenance and improvement.

**High-impact, low-cost refreshes:**

- **Paint.** A fresh coat of paint on one feature wall transforms the space. Choose a colour that complements your brand and photographs well (customers post photos online — your walls are backdrop).
- **Seating fabric.** Reupholster or replace cushions and seat pads. Fabric absorbs smells and stains over time. New cushion covers cost far less than new furniture.
- **Lighting.** Swap harsh overhead lights for warmer, softer options. Pendant lights over tables, warm-white LEDs, and dimmable fixtures for evening trade change the atmosphere without structural work.
- **Artwork and decoration.** Rotate artwork quarterly. Partner with local artists — they get exposure, you get free or low-cost art that changes regularly. Remove anything that has been on the wall for more than a year.
- **Plants.** Living plants improve air quality and aesthetics. Choose low-maintenance varieties (pothos, snake plants, succulents) and assign watering to the cleaning schedule.

**Maintenance that prevents decline:**

- Fix wobbly tables and chairs immediately. Customers will avoid them, reducing your usable seating.
- Replace cracked or chipped crockery. Damaged cups and plates undermine the quality message your coffee is trying to send.
- Clean windows weekly, inside and out. Natural light is your best design feature — dirty windows dim it.
- Check toilets twice daily for cleanliness and supplies. A dirty toilet is the single most damaging thing a customer can encounter. It overrides every other positive impression.

Design refreshes are not cosmetic indulgences. They are maintenance of the environment that generates your revenue.

sections.heading: Outdoor Seating Potential
sections.body:
Outdoor seating extends your capacity without extending your lease. A well-managed outside area can increase revenue by ten to twenty percent during warmer months.

**Licensing and permissions:**

If your outdoor seating is on the public pavement, you need a pavement licence from your local council. Check your local municipality's specific requirements — they vary by area. Conditions typically cover the number of tables, the hours of use, accessibility requirements, and noise.

If your outdoor area is on your own private land (a garden, a courtyard, a forecourt), you generally do not need a specific licence, but check your lease and planning conditions.

**Making outdoor seating work:**

- **Furniture.** Invest in durable, weather-resistant furniture that does not blow over in the wind. Cheap, lightweight tables and chairs become projectiles in a gust and look shabby within months. Metal or hardwood furniture lasts years and looks professional.
- **Shelter.** Parasols for sun and retractable awnings for rain extend the usable season significantly. Customers will sit outside in light rain under a good awning. They will not sit outside in any weather without shade.
- **Heating.** Outdoor heaters extend the season into spring and autumn. Electric infrared heaters are more energy-efficient and more environmentally friendly than gas patio heaters. Position them where they are most effective — overhead, directed at seating areas.
- **Service.** Decide whether outdoor customers order at the counter or are served at the table. Table service outdoors creates a more relaxed, premium experience and increases dwell time and spend. Counter service is simpler operationally.

**Maintaining the space:**

Outdoor areas need daily maintenance: sweeping, wiping tables after rain, removing litter, checking that furniture is stable and clean. An unkempt outdoor area creates a worse impression than no outdoor area at all. Assign outdoor maintenance to the opening checklist and the mid-afternoon reset.

Consider your neighbours. Noise, smoke, litter, and late-night use can create complaints. Be a good neighbour and your outdoor area will be sustainable long-term.

sections.heading: The Quarterly Deep Clean
sections.body:
Daily cleaning maintains the surface. A quarterly deep clean reaches everything that daily cleaning misses — the tops of shelves, behind equipment, inside ventilation ducts, underneath counters, the grout between tiles.

**Planning the deep clean:**

Schedule it on your quietest trading day, or close early to allow time. A deep clean takes four to eight hours depending on the size of your cafe. You need the full team or a dedicated cleaning crew.

**The deep clean checklist:**

**Kitchen and prep area:**
- Pull out all equipment and clean behind and underneath (ovens, fridges, dishwashers)
- Degrease extractor fans and hood filters
- Deep clean oven interiors
- Descale all kettles, coffee machines, and water boilers
- Clean fridge and freezer interiors, check door seals
- Wash walls and splash-backs
- Clean and sanitise all drains
- Check for and treat any pest evidence

**Front of house:**
- Clean all light fittings and fixtures
- Wash windows inside and out
- Deep clean upholstery and soft furnishings
- Shampoo or steam-clean any carpeted areas
- Clean all skirting boards and door frames
- Sanitise all door handles and touch points
- Polish or treat any wooden surfaces

**Toilets:**
- Descale all fixtures
- Clean grouting
- Check and repair any sealant
- Deep clean hand dryers or replace paper towel dispensers
- Repaint if necessary

**Storage and back of house:**
- Empty and clean all shelves
- Dispose of expired stock
- Check for pest evidence
- Clean floors including under shelving
- Reorganise storage zones if they have drifted

**After the deep clean:**

Take photographs. These serve as the benchmark for the next quarter — you can see exactly what "clean" looks like and compare. They also demonstrate due diligence for food safety inspections.

The quarterly deep clean is not glamorous work. But it prevents the slow accumulation of grime that turns a clean cafe into a dirty one so gradually that nobody inside notices — until a customer does, or an inspector does, and by then the damage is done.
